coredata.dc:title: A novel “off-on” ratiometric fluorescent probe based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots for silver ion and glutathione assays

coredata.dc:description: Dual-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with yellow fluorescence was prepared by one-step solvothermal method with o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and quercetin (QR) as precursors, and its preparation conditions (like effects of different dosage ratios of QR and OPD, time and temperature) were investigated in detail. Meanwhile, the N-CDs showed two emission peaks at 465 and 568 nm under the excitation wavelength of 395 nm and its quantum yield was 11.3 % using rhodamine 6G as a reference standard. Then the N-CDs as a novel ratiometric fluorescence probe (RF-probe) was employed to selectively sense Ag+ according to its fluorescence quenching, which exhibited a much wider linear range (0.05–80.0 μM) and lower limit of detection (LOD, 17.8 nM) than the Y-CDs (yellow carbon dots) and BY-CDs (physically mixing the B-CDs (blue carbon dots) and Y-CDs) did. Interestingly, glutathione (GSH) could recover the fluorescence of the N-CDs@Ag+ system. Based on this, an off-on switching system was constructed to monitor GSH with the linear range of 0.015–0.48 μM and low LOD of 5.5 nM. Furthermore, the effect of silver ion doses on the RF-probe construction and the sensing mechanisms of the N-CDs@Ag+ and N-CDs@Ag+-GSH systems were also further discussed. Meanwhile, the high recoveries in water and fruit samples (94.6 %–107.9 % and 91.3 %–108.7 %) were observed, which proved that the N-CDs and N-CDs@Ag+ probes were reliable for the Ag+ and GSH assays, respectively.

head.ce:title: A novel “off-on” ratiometric fluorescent probe based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots for silver ion and glutathione assays

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dual-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with yellow fluorescence was prepared by one-step solvothermal method with o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and quercetin (QR) as precursors, and its preparation conditions (like effects of different dosage ratios of QR and OPD, time and temperature) were investigated in detail. Meanwhile, the N-CDs showed two emission peaks at 465 and 568 nm under the excitation wavelength of 395 nm and its quantum yield was 11.3 % using rhodamine 6G as a reference standard. Then the N-CDs as a novel ratiometric fluorescence probe (RF-probe) was employed to selectively sense Ag+ according to its fluorescence quenching, which exhibited a much wider linear range (0.05–80.0 μM) and lower limit of detection (LOD, 17.8 nM) than the Y-CDs (yellow carbon dots) and BY-CDs (physically mixing the B-CDs (blue carbon dots) and Y-CDs) did. Interestingly, glutathione (GSH) could recover the fluorescence of the N-CDs@Ag+ system. Based on this, an off-on switching system was constructed to monitor GSH with the linear range of 0.015–0.48 μM and low LOD of 5.5 nM. Furthermore, the effect of silver ion doses on the RF-probe construction and the sensing mechanisms of the N-CDs@Ag+ and N-CDs@Ag+-GSH systems were also further discussed. Meanwhile, the high recoveries in water and fruit samples (94.6 %–107.9 % and 91.3 %–108.7 %) were observed, which proved that the N-CDs and N-CDs@Ag+ probes were reliable for the Ag+ and GSH assays, respectively.

Dual-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots (the N-CDs) was prepared by one-step solvothermal method with o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and quercetin (QR) as raw materials. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs at 568 nm was selectively quenched by Ag+ based on the static quenching. Furthermore, in the presence of GSH, the fluorescence of the N-CDs@Ag+ system gradually recovered due to the formation of more stable complex between Ag+ and GSH. Thus, the N-CDs could be used as a novel “off-on” ratiometric fluorescent probe (F 568/F 465) to monitor sensitively Ag+ and GSH, and corresponding LOD values were estimated to be 17.8 and 5.5 nM. Besides, the method was extended to the assays of Ag+ and GSH in real samples with satisfactory results.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: As a vital noble metal, silver (Ag) has been widely used in our daily lives and mass manufacturing industries [1]. On the one hand, silver ions (Ag+) have been applied in water storage, water purification and treatment of infections owing to its unique antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties [2,3]. On the other hand, Ag+ is also widely used in the fields of catalysts, electrodes, antimicrobial agents [4–6], because it possesses special physical, chemical and optical properties. However, release of Ag+ into rivers and streams may cause pollution in the water, soil, and even the food chain [7]. Meanwhile, if Ag+ is ingested by human, it not only can disrupt homeostasis and metabolism, but also can rapidly make enzyme inactivation which will cause neurological and pulmonary damages, renal failure, and even death [8]. As a result, the World Health Organization stipulated that the amount of Ag+ in drinking water should not exceed 0.5 μM [9]. Glutathione (GSH), a non-protein antioxidant tripeptide containing sulfhydryl groups, is widely found in fruits and vegetables. As GSH is ingested by the body, it can prevent or defer disease by neutralizing reactive oxygen species (ROS) produced in the body during physiological activity [10]. Likewise, it also plays an important role in maintaining gene regulation, intracellular redox status, detoxification and metabolism [11]. In addition, the concentrations of GSH are ranging from 0.5 to 10.0 mM in normal cells [12]. However, the abnormal levels of GSH are strongly associated with many diseases. For instance, the low levels of GSH might cause harm to the stomach and liver, while its excessive levels would lead to Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, leukopenia, neurological disease and cancer [13–16]. In this view, the development of rapid and highly sensitive methods for the detections of Ag+ and GSH should be urgent and necessary.

Traditional detection methods of Ag+ and GSH included atomic absorption spectrometry [17], colorimetric analysis [18], high-performance liquid chromatography [19], electrochemical process [20], and so on. Although the methods had the advantages of high sensitivity and accurate measurement, they also exhibited a series of defects, such as narrow linear ranges, high detection interference, complex equipment operation, specific electrodes, or high cost. Compared with traditional detection techniques, fluorescence spectroscopy method exhibited the advantages of wide linear ranges, high selectivity, simple operation, and low-cost testing. Therefore, in recent years, an increasing number of fluorescent probe materials have attracted widespread attention, such as organic molecular, metal organic frameworks, covalent organic frameworks, metal nanoclusters, semiconductor quantum dots and carbon dots (CDs) [21,22]. However, among them, CDs had the advantages of excellent water solubility [23], good biocompatibility [24], low toxicity [25,26], and high light stability [27], thereupon it was not only employed in the fields of medical treatment [28–30] and sustainable development [31,32], but also could be used as sensors [33]. For example, a series of detection switches with single emission peak for Ag+ and GSH have been studied [34–36]. However, single-emission fluorescent probes were susceptible to interference due to background noise, matrix effects, and variation concentration of the probe [37]. Fortunately, ratio fluorescence probes (RF-probes) could eliminate above effects, which would cause that more accurate examination results were obtained by the RF-probes. Therefore, some research groups developed RF-probes to detect Ag+ and/or GSH. For instance, long-wavelength emission nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was developed for intrinsic ratiometric detections of Ag+ and GSH in the linear ranges of 0–140.0 and 0–120.0 μM, and corresponding LOD values were calculated to be 0.12 and 0.38 μM, respectively [37]. The CDs@BiL-Eu-MOF was successfully synthesized and employed to visual detect Ag+ with a wide linear range of 0–660.0 μM and high LOD of 2.1 μM [38]. The multi-nanomaterial (CD/CdTe QD@ZIF-8) was used as a RF-probe for the Ag+ assay with low LOD (1.49 nM) [39]. Alhazznai et al. [40] synthesized a new Ag-RF/N-CD fluorescence probe, and the RF-probe could sensitively monitor GSH with a linear range of 0.05–70.0 μM and low LOD (15.0 nM). Lately, a new RF-probe (BY-CDs) was established by physical mixing of blue carbon dots (B-CDs) and yellow carbon dots (Y-CDs), and the BY-CDs was constructed the logic gate of Cu2+ and GSH [41]. Despite the established fluorescent sensors exhibited high accuracy and excellent selectivity for the Ag+ and/or GSH sensing, they still had some disadvantages (like high cost, high toxicity, time consuming synthesis process, or high LOD). In a word, there were very few reports about ratio fluorescent switches for detecting Ag+ and GSH, meanwhile more researches should be needed on this aspect of constructing RF-probes with low cost and low LOD.

In this work, dual-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with low cost was fabricated by quercetin (QR) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as raw materials via one-step solvothermal method. Then, the N-CDs as a novel RF-probe, was successfully used for gauging Ag+ and GSH with low LOD. Moreover, the effect on the detection sensitivity of Ag+ under different dosage ratios of raw materials and the sensing mechanisms of the N-CDs@Ag+ and N-CDs@Ag+-GSH systems were discussed in detail.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental section
Reagents
ce:section.ce:para: All reagents were described in the support information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Instrumentations
ce:section.ce:para: The sizes and morphologies of the N-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) with a JEM-2100F (JEOL, Japan), X-ray diffraction (XRD) with a DX-2700BH (Dandong, China), and confocal laser Raman spectrometer with a AIpha 300 access (WITec, Germany), respectively. ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used to analyze surface functional property of the N-CDs. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded by Nicolet-6700 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with KBr pellet technique. All fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by F-4700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) particle size and Zeta potential of the N-CDs were obtained by Zeta potential analyzer (Nano ZSP, Malvern, England).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparations of N-CDs, B-CDs, Y-CDs and BY-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis step of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was described as follows: 0.054 g of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and 0.060 g of quercetin (QR) were dissolved into 10.0 mL ultrapure water. After 10 min of sonication, and then the mixed solution was transferred to a 30.0 mL of Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 4 h. After the reactor was naturally cooled to room temperature, an orange-red suspension was obtained. Thereafter, the suspension was filtered by 0.22 μm of microporous filter membrane. Subsequently, the filtered solution was dialyzed at 25 °C for 24 h through a dialysis bag (500 Da) to obtain purificatory N-CDs, and the obtained N-CDs was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for following experiments. Besides, blue carbon dots (B-CDs) and yellow carbon dots (Y-CDs) were prepared in the same manner as the N-CDs using QR or OPD as precursors, respectively. BY-CDs was obtained by physically mixing the B-CDs and Y-CDs in equal proportion. The B-CDs and Y-CDs were purified in the same way as the N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield measurement of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yields (QYs) of all CDs samples were calculated based on equation (1) (Taking rhodamine 6G as a reference standard (Φ s = 90 %)).
(1)
Φ x

Φ s

where Φ, A, I, and η express the quantum yield, absorbance, integral area of target fluorescence intensity and refractive index of the solvent, respectively. The subscript s and x indicate the reference standard and CDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of Ag+ and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: For the assay of Ag+, a series of different volumes of Ag+ (3.0 × 10−4 mol/L) were poured into 0.1 mL of the N-CDs solution, and the mixed solution was diluted to 3.0 mL with Britton-Robinson (BR, pH = 7.0) and incubated for 2 min, and then their fluorescence spectra were recorded from 415 to 700 nm (λ ex = 395 nm, slit widths of excitation and emission both were set at 10 nm). For the assay of GSH, 0.1 mL of the N-CDs solution was diluted to 3.0 mL with BR solution (pH = 7.0), then 80.0 μL of Ag+ (3.0 × 10−3 mol/L) was added and reacted for 5 min to establish the RF-probe (the N-CDs@Ag+ system). Afterward a series of different volumes of GSH (4.0 × 10−5 mol/L) were poured into above N-CDs@Ag+ solution and incubated for 2 min, and corresponding fluorescence spectra were mensurated with the same test parameters as Ag+ admeasurement. In the selective and anti-interference tests, the developed N-CDs or N-CDs@Ag+ probe was investigated under the same experiment conditions according to the selected substances without and with the addition of Ag+ or GSH. All experiments were measured three times in parallel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determinations of Ag+ and GSH in actual samples
Determination of Ag+ in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Drinking water, tap water and lake water were selected as the actual samples, which were originated from pure water processing plants at China West Normal University, water delivery company of Nanchong city and YingXi lake in China West Normal University, respectively. Each of the three water samples collected was purified through 0.22 μm of microporous filter membrane, and the solutions obtained were used as the respective stock solution. After 50.0 μL of stock solution was mixed uniformly with 0.1 mL of the N-CDs solution, the mixed solution was fixed to 3.0 mL with BR solution (pH = 7.0). Later, a series of different volumes of Ag+ standard solution (3.0 × 10−3 mol/L) were added to the mixed solution (known concentrations of 6.0, 36.0, 66.0 μM for Ag+). Finally, the concentrations of Ag+ in the actual samples were performed according to the testing procedure of Section 2.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of GSH in fruits samples
ce:section.ce:para: Purchased tomatoes, red grapes and oranges were selected as the real samples from local farmer markets of Nanchong city. Firstly, all three fruit samples were washed three times with ultrapure water and dried naturally. Subsequently, the air-dried samples were juiced and filtered through the qualitative filter paper. Then, above crude product solutions were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min and filtered through 0.22 μm of microporous filter membrane to receive clarified fruit juices as each stock solution [42]. Later, 15.0 μL of each stock solution was taken and mixed homogeneously with the N-CDs@Ag+ probe which was prepared by the procedure of Section 2.5. Afterward, a series of different doses of GSH standard solution (4.0 × 10−5 mol/L) were poured into the mixed solution (known concentrations of 55.0, 180.0, 320.0 nM for GSH). Finally, all experiments were done based on the test parameters in Section 2.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical analysis
ce:section.ce:para: All analytical data were obtained by the average of three determinations. Origin 2022 software was employed to process experimental data and graph production. The values of t-test were calculated by SPSS 22.0 analysis software to determine whether there was a significant difference between the two methods. Value of p < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Optimized synthesis conditions of N-CDs
Effect on the detection sensitivity of Ag + under different dosage ratios of QR and OPD
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence emission peak of the N-CDs located at 568 nm originated from the Y-CDs with only OPD as raw material, while its emission peak at 465 nm was mainly attributed to the B-CDs with only QR as carbon source. To obtain the N-CDs with excellent optical properties and optimal detection performance for Ag+, then different dosage ratios of QR and OPD were discussed. That is, the dosage of QR was fixed at 0.2 mmol and the OPD dosages were increased from 0 to 0.6 mmol. As illustrated in Fig. S2a, the fluorescence emission peak of the N-CDs at 568 nm was almost absent when the dose of OPD was too low, while its emission peak at 465 nm almost disappeared as the dose of OPD was too high. To investigate effect on the detection sensitivity of Ag+ under different dosage ratios of QR and OPD, 0.05 μM of Ag+ (it was the minimum concentration to be identified by the N-CDs@Ag+ system) was added to the prepared N-CDs with different dosage ratios of QR and OPD, and corresponding varying degree of fluorescence quenching was displayed in Fig. S2b. As demonstrated in Fig. 1 a, the value of ΔF 568/F 465 (ΔF 568/F 465
F 568,0/F 465,0-F 568/F 465, F 465,0, F 568,0, F 465 and F 568 denote the fluorescence intensities of the N-CDs at 465 and 568 nm without and with the addition of Ag+, respectively) was maximum when the dose ratio of QR and OPD was set at 1:2.5, indicating that the prepared N-CDs with the dose ratio of QR and OPD (n QR:n OPD = 1:2.5) exhibited the highest sensitivity to the assay of Ag+. Besides, the average dynamic light scattering (DLS) particle sizes of the prepared N-CDs with different dosage ratios of QR and OPD were listed in Fig. 1b, which were obtained by corresponding DLS particle sizes (Fig. S3a–g)). The detection sensitivity of Ag+ was highest when the average DLS particle size of the N-CDs was smallest (143.4 nm) (Fig. 1a–b). This was probably because the smaller average DLS particle size hinted the more uniform distribution of particles in the N-CDs solution [43], so that it could facilitate the binding of Ag+ with the N-CDs. Furthermore, the magnitude of the Zeta potential seemed to be somewhat related to the level of sensitivity as well. As conveyed in Fig. 1c, the Zeta potentials of the obtained N-CDs exhibited a tendency of gradually decreasing (from 1.13 to −36.25 mV) and then increasing (from −36.25 to −6.80 mV) as the dosage ratios of QR and OPD increased. Interestingly, the N-CDs with the smallest Zeta potential (−36.25 mV) was corresponded to the highest sensitivity for the Ag+ sensing. It was highly likely that the N-CDs with smaller Zeta potentials contained more negatively charged groups (such as carboxyl and amino groups) on its surface, which might be more conducive to the binding of the N-CDs with Ag+ [44]. Therefore, the optimum n QR:n OPD was set at 1:2.5 for the N-CDs preparation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of synthesis time and temperature
ce:section.ce:para: The reaction time and temperature of the N-CDs were also investigated to search the most sensitive detection of Ag+. As depicted in Fig. S4a, the fluorescence emission peaks of the N-CDs at 465 nm disappeared at too short or too long synthesis time. Moreover, the prepared N-CDs with the reaction time of 4 h exhibited the maximum ΔF 568/F 465 value (which was calculated based on the fluorescence intensities of the N-CDs at 465 and 568 nm without and with the addition of Ag+ (Fig. S4a–b)), indicating that the N-CDs responding to Ag+ was the most sensitive at this time (Fig. S4c). Thus, the optimum reaction time for the N-CDs preparation was kept at 4 h. In addition, the optimized reaction temperature of the prepared N-CDs was obtained at 180 °C (Fig. S4d–f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical properties of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM (transmission electron microscopy), HR-TEM (high-resolution TEM) techniques, XRD (X-ray diffraction) and Raman spectroscopy were employed to manifest the sizes and morphologies of the N-CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 2 a, The N-CDs exhibited a rounded spherical shape and significant aggregation wasn't found in the aqueous phase. The diameter of the N-CDs was mostly in the range of 1–3 nm and its average diameter was 1.89 nm. The lattice spacing of the N-CDs was about 0.18 nm (Fig. 2b), which was corresponded to the (100) plane of graphitized carbon [45–47]. Likewise, XRD pattern of the N-CDs displayed an evident peak at around 2θ = 23°, which was attributed to the (002) plane of the graphite structure (Fig. S5). As shown in Fig. S6, the Raman spectrum of the N-CDs exhibited two bands at 1388 cm−1 (D band) and 1560 cm−1 (G band), corresponding to sp2 and sp3 hybrid orbitals of carbon atom, respectively. Meanwhile, the unique G band peak of the N-CDs reconfirmed that it possessed a high extent of graphitization. Besides, the average diameters of the B-CDs and Y-CDs were 2.18 and 2.20 nm (Fig. S7a–S8a), and lattice patterns couldn't be observed in their HR-TEM images (Fig. S7b–S8b).

The composition and valence states of elements in the N-CDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and corresponding full spectrum and high-resolution energy spectra were shown in Fig. 2c–f. Three significant peaks at 285.0, 400.0 and 533.0 eV were correlated with C 1s (74.2 %), N 1s (14.4 %) and O 1s (11.4 %), respectively (Fig. 2c). The binding energy peaks of 284.8, 286.0, 286.8 and 288.2 eV in the C 1s XPS spectra were attributed to C–C/CC, C–N, C–O and CO/CN, respectively [48] (Fig. 2d). As depicted in Fig. 2e, the XPS high-resolution spectra of N 1s were divided into four binding energy peaks located at 399.3, 400.4, 401.0 and 401.7 eV, corresponding to pyridine N, amino N, pyrrole N and graphite N, respectively [49,50]. And the XPS high-resolution spectra of O 1s at 532.6, 531.1 and 534.9 eV were in agreement with C–O, O–CO and CO [51] (Fig. 2f). Moreover, to further investigate the surface functional groups of the N-CDs, corresponding FT-IR spectrum was recorded and analyzed (Fig. S9a). The absorption peaks at 2904–3025,1402 and 617-881 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration peaks of C–H, CO and C–H (out-of-plane bending vibration) respectively, hinting that the N-CDs had a polyaromatic structure [52]. Meanwhile, near 1135 and 1405 cm−1, the stretching vibration peaks of C–N and CO–NH were presented [53,54], hinting that the N-CDs was formed successfully. Besides, different fluorescence decay times of the B-CDs, Y-CDs and N-CDs also could reconfirm the successful synthesis of the N-CDs (Fig. S10 and Table S1).

In addition, its optical properties were also explored by the fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra. The fluorescence emission peaks of the N-CDs at 465 and 568 nm were observed under the excitation wavelength of 395 nm, and it showed orange-yellow in daylight and emitted yellow fluorescence under UV light of 365 nm (Fig. S11a), indicating the N-CDs was synthesized successfully. Moreover, there was a clear excitation-dependent phenomenon for the N-CDs at near 465 nm (Fig. S11b). Besides, the characteristic UV–vis absorption peaks of the N-CDs at 230 and 289 nm might originate from π-π∗ transition of multiple conjugated C–C bonds in the benzene ring and n-π∗ transition of C–O/C–N bond [55] (Fig. S9b), reconfirming that the N-CDs was prepared successfully. Finally, the quantum yield of the N-CDs was calculated to be 11.3 % using rhodamine 6G as a reference standard. Meanwhile, the quantum yields of the B-CDs and Y-CDs were also calculated to be 2.8 % and 29.5 % based on the same method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The assay performance of N-CDs, BY-CDs and Y-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To acquire the best testing conditions for Ag+, time (t), temperature (T), pH and excitation wavelength (λ ex) were studied. As described in Fig. S12a–d, the optimal t, T, pH and λ ex were kept at 2.0 min, 20 °C, 7.0 and 395 nm.

The N-CDs was employed to construct the RF-probe with different concentrations of Ag+ under optimal detection conditions. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence intensities of the RF-probe at 465 nm were basically unchanged, while its fluorescence intensities at 568 nm gradually decreased with increasing the concentrations of Ag+. The fluorescence intensity ratio values (F 568/F 465) demonstrated a good negative correlation with the concentrations of Ag+ in the range of 0.05–80.0 μΜ, and corresponding linear fitting equation was F 568/F 465 = −0.0122 × C Ag + + 2.0171 (R 2 = 0.9958) (Fig. 3b), while its LOD was calculated to be 17.8 nM based on the equation (3) σ/k (σ expresses the standard deviation of the blank measurements without the addition of Ag+). Besides, to ascertain the optimal RF-probe, the BY-CDs as a RF-probe was also investigated for the assay of Ag+. As depicted in Fig. S13a–b, the fluorescence intensities of the BY-CDs at 465 and 568 nm both weakened gradually with the increase of the Ag+ concentrations (from 0 to 8.15 μΜ), and the ratio values of F 568/F 465 were positively correlated with Ag + concentrations in the range of 0.15–8.15 μΜ (F 568/F 465 = 0.0103 × C Ag + + 1.8296, R 2 = 0.9995). The LOD of the BY-CDs@Ag+ system was counted to be 52.3 nM. Considering both the linear range and LOD, it was obvious that the N-CDs showed simpler synthesis process and better detection performance for Ag+ than the BY-CDs did. In addition, to further demonstrate that the RF-probe (the N-CDs) had better detection properties for Ag+, the single emission peak probe (the Y-CDs) was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S14a–b, the fluorescence intensities of the Y-CDs at 568 nm decreased with the increase of Ag + concentrations. And the linear fitting equation was F 568/F 568,0 = −0.0230 × C Ag + + 0.9591 (R 2 = 0.9845), and corresponding LOD was calculated to be 34.8 nM. Moreover, it was found the larger average DLS particle size (431.2 nm) (it was obtained by corresponding DLS particle sizes (Fig. S15a1-a3 )) and higher Zeta potential (−7.65 mV) of the Y-CDs than those of the N-CDs (Fig. S16a–b), indicating that the Y-CDs (the single emission peak probe) exhibited lower sensitivity for the Ag+ sensing than the N-CDs (the RF-probe) did. In short, the N-CDs as a novel RF-probe not only had simply synthesis process, but also exhibited sensitively responding to Ag+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of GSH using the N-CDs@Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S17a, the fluorescence intensities of the N-CDs at 465 and 568 nm had been little changes in the presence of GSH, which would result in their average DLS particle sizes with minor changes (They were obtained by corresponding DLS particle sizes (Fig. S18)) (Fig. S17b). Thus, a new RF-probe (the N-CDs@Ag+) was established to monitor GSH.

To probe into the N-CDs@Ag+ with excellent response to GSH, the additive amounts of Ag+ were explored. 15.0 nM of GSH (The minimum concentration was identified by the N-CDs@Ag+-GSH system) was poured into the N-CDs@Ag+ with different concentrations of Ag+. As shown in Fig. S19, the value of ΔF 568/F 465 (ΔF 568/F 465
F 568,0/F 465,0-F 568/F 465, F 465,0, F 568,0, F 465 and F 568 denote the fluorescence intensities of the N-CDs@Ag+ at 465 and 568 nm in the absence and presence of GSH, respectively) was maximum when the concentration of Ag+ was set at 80.0 μΜ, hinting that the N-CDs with the addition of 80.0 μΜ Ag+ revealed the highest sensitivity to the GSH sensing. Nevertheless, the values of ΔF 568/F 465 reduced when excessive Ag+ (more than 80.0 μΜ) was added to the N-CDs solution, probably because GSH would preferentially bind with the free Ag+ resulting in no fluorescence recovery. Therefore, the N-CDs with 80.0 μΜ Ag+ was established as the optimum condition for the detection of GSH.

Under optimal assay condition, the fluorescence intensities of the N-CDs@Ag+ at 465 and 568 nm slightly reduced and gradually recovered with increasing GSH concentrations (Fig. 4 a). In the linear range of 15.0–480.0 nM, the values of F 568/F 465 showed a good positive correlation with different concentrations of GSH (F 568/F 465 = 0.0033 × C GSH + 1.1473, R 2 = 0.9993) (Fig. 4b), and the LOD of the N-CDs@Ag+-GSH system was calculated to be 5.5 nM using the 3σ/k method (σ denotes the standard deviation of the blank measurements without the addition of GSH).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of the N-CDs and N-CDs@Ag+ systems
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the N-CDs was studied by adding different kinds of metal ions (such as K+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Mg2+, NH4 +, Mn2+, Cr6+, Al3+, Ba2+, Fe3+, Na+, Ni2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+). As presented in Fig. S20, with adding Ag+ to the N-CDs solution, the value of F 568/F 465 reduced evidently. While other metal ions had negligible effects on the corresponding F 568/F 465 values. The result indicated that the N-CDs was virtually undisturbed by coexisting metal ions (Fig. S20). As such, the N-CDs had excellent selectivity and anti-interference.

GSH was usually found in fruits and vegetables, so some closely related substances were selected to investigate the selectivity and anti-interference of the N-CDs@Ag+-GSH system, which included some common metal ions (K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Zn2+ and Na+), amino acids (L-Cys, L-His, L-Thr, L-Lys and L-Ile), sugars (maltose, sucrose and glucose), vitamins (VB2, VB6 and VC) and other common compounds (orthoboric acid, CA, hesperiden, naringin, choline and folic acid). GSH and DL-Cys both made the F 568/F 465 value amplify, which indicated that the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs@Ag+ system at 568 nm recovered, but GSH could restore its fluorescence intensity at 568 nm to the greatest extent (Fig. S21a). Besides, the results of Fig. S21b demonstrated that the N-CDs@Ag+ system was essentially free of interference from above co-existing substances for the GSH assay. Therefore, the N-CDs@Ag+ system (the RF-probe) possessed good selectivity and anti-interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism
The fluorescence quenching mechanism of the N-CDs@Ag+ system
ce:section.ce:para:
The main types of fluorescence quenching include static quenching, dynamic quenching, electron transfer (ET), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), and internal filtration effect (IFE) [56–59]. The fluorescence quenching of the N-CDs by Ag+ could be ruled out FRET and IFE due to no overlapping between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Ag+ and the fluorescence excitation/emission spectrum of the N-CDs (Fig. 5 a). Likewise, the UV–vis absorption of the N-CDs at 230 and 289 nm both decreased with the addition of Ag+ (Fig. S22), which was mainly attributed to the formation of a non-fluorescent complex between the N-CDs and Ag+. The result indicated that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the N-CDs-Ag+ system wasn't IFE or ET. Besides, there was no significant changes of fluorescence decay curves between the N-CDs and N-CDs@Ag+ systems (Fig. 5b), and corresponding the average fluorescence lifetimes were estimated to be1.87 and 1.81 ns (Table S1), suggesting that the fluorescence quenching of the N-CDs by Ag+ didn't belong to dynamic quenching and FRET [60]. Above studies hinted that static quenching was most likely the quenching mechanism for the N-CDs@Ag+ system, which could also be confirmed though the Stern-Volmer equation (2) [61].
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 + K sv
[ Q ]
= 1 +
K q
τ
[ Q ]
In the Eq. (2), F 0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of the N-CDs at 568 nm without and with the addition of Ag+; K sv and K q refer to the Stern-Volmer constant and the quenching rate constant; [Q] denotes the concentration of Ag+ and τ represents the average fluorescence lifetime of the N-CDs (1.87 ns). The values of F 0/F and the concentrations of Ag + exhibited good linear correlations at three different temperatures (Fig. 5c). The K SV values of the N-CDs@Ag+ system decreased gradually with increasing the temperatures (Table S2), which was a typical feature of static quenching [62]. Moreover, its K q values (8.50, 8.24 and 1.44 × 1012 L·moL−1·s−1 for 283, 293 and 303 K, respectively) were far greater than the theoretical diffusion-controlled limit (∼1010 L·moL−1·s−1), reconfirming that the fluorescence quenching of the N-CDs by Ag+ belonged to static quenching mechanism. In addition, to further confirm the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the N-CDs@Ag+ system, corresponding FTIR spectra, the average DLS particle sizes (which were obtained by corresponding DLS particle sizes (Fig. S23a–b), and Zeta potential were also explored. As presented in Fig. 5d, the FTIR absorption peak relative intensity of CO significantly reduced, suggesting that Ag+ was somewhat bound to the carboxyl groups on the surface of the N-CDs. Surprisingly, the changes of Zeta potential showed consistent results with the changes in their FTIR, i.e., the Zeta potential of the N-CDs increased dramatically from −36.25 to +7.20 mV in the presence of Ag+ (Fig. 5e), which was mainly attributed to the consumption of the carboxyl or other negative groups on the surface of the N-CDs. The results reconfirmed the successful combination between the N-CDs and Ag+. Furthermore, when a certain dose of Ag+ was added to the N-CDs solution, its average DLS particle sizes increased sharply from 152.2 to 1032.9 nm, probably because a non-fluorescent complex with the larger average DLS particle size was formed between Ag+ and the N-CDs (Fig. 5f). In a conclusion, all of the above studies suggested that the fluorescence quenching of the N-CDs by Ag+ belonged to static quenching mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensing mechanism of the N-CDs@Ag+-GSH system
ce:section.ce:para: When a certain dose of GSH was added to the N-CDs@Ag+ solution, corresponding FTIR absorption peak relative intensity of CO markedly enhanced (Fig. 5d), which was a symbol of the carboxyl groups on the surface of the N-CDs recovering to its original form, indicating that the combination of Ag+ with the N-CDs was broken. Likewise, the Zeta potentials of the N-CDs@Ag+ reduced (from 7.20 to 0.71 mV) with the addition of GSH (Fig. 5e), mainly owing to increasing number of negatively charged groups (such as carboxyl groups). Moreover, it was also found that the UV–vis absorption spectra of the N-CDs@Ag+ at 230 and 289 nm recovered to some extent in the presence of GSH (Fig. S22), possibly because forming GSH-Ag+ complex had better stability than the N-CDs@Ag+ did [63]. Besides, the average DLS particle sizes of the N-CDs@Ag+ (which were obtained by corresponding DLS particle sizes (Fig. S23b–c) diminished markedly (from 1032.9 to 485.4 nm) by adding GSH (Fig. 5f), which was the result of removing Ag+ from the N-CDs@Ag+ complex [64]. Thus, the fluorescence recovery of the N-CDs@Ag+ system at 568 nm by GSH was attributed to the formation of more stable complex between Ag+ and GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of Ag+ and GSH in water and fruit samples
Comparison of the established method with other reported methods
ce:section.ce:para: The assay of Ag+ by the prepared N-CDs in comparison with other reported methods was elaborated in Table S7. Based on the linear range and LOD, the N-CDs not only had the widest linear range except for the S, N-CQDs and Ch-CQDs-RhB (corresponding linear ranges were 0–250.0 and 22.0–220.0 μM for the Ag+ detection), but also had the highest sensitivity in addition to the B-CQDs and Dual-QDs (1.4 and 7.7 nM for the Ag+ assay). However, the three fluorescent probes (such as the Ch-CQDs-RhB (F 580/F 412), Dual-QDs (F 570/F 540) and N-CDs (F 568/F 465)) were constructed as fluorescence ratio sensing platforms for the Ag+ sensing, which were advantageous in terms of single-emission peak fluorescent probes (like the S, N-CQDs and B-CQDs). But the synthesis processes of both Ch-CQDs-RhB and Dual-QDs probes were complicated, so the developed RF-probe (the N-CDs) had certain advantages in the detection of Ag+. As shown in Table S8, compared to the reported methods (like the N-CDs@Hg2+, N, S-Cdots-MnO2, BO-CDs, SNCDs, RCDs and De-CDs@MnO2 probes), the established method (the N-CDs@Ag+ probe) showed the narrowest linear range (0.015–0.48 μM for the GSH measurement), but it had the lowest LOD (5.5 nM) except for the RCDs (1.6 nM) and BO-CDs (3.33 nM). Besides, the BO-CDs (F 570/F 420), De-CDs@MnO2 (F 435/F 516) and N-CDs@Ag+ (F 568/F 465) were set up the RF-probe for the GSH sensing, which might eliminate external influences (such as background noise, matrix effects and variations in the probe concentration). However, the established N-CDs probe was capable of detecting both Ag+ and GSH, hinting that the method had a wide range of applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: In summary, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (the N-CDs) with bright-yellow fluorescence was successfully synthetized by one-pot hydrothermal method with OPD and QR as raw materials. Meanwhile, the optimized preparation conditions of the N-CDs were discussed and the N-CDs was also characterized by TEM, XRD, Raman, XPS, FT-IR and UV–vis absorption spectra, respectively. The optimized N-CDs displayed two fluorescence emission peaks at 465 and 568 nm under the excitation wavelength of 395 nm, so it could be used as a novel off-on ratiometric fluorescent probe (RF-probe) for the determinations of Ag+ and GSH with low LOD (17.8 and 5.5 nM). Moreover, the RF-probe showed better detection capability for Ag+ than the Y-CDs and BY-CDs did. The results of UV–vis absorption and FTIR spectra, fluorescence decay curves, Stern-Volmer diagram, Zeta potential and the average DLS particle sizes suggested that the sensing mechanisms of the N-CDs@Ag+ and N-CDs@Ag+-GSH systems belonged to static quenching and the formation of stable complex. In addition, its good anti-interference ability impelled it to be efficiently employed to measure Ag+ and GSH in water and fruit samples, and the high sensitivity, excellent selectivity and simple synthesis steps might make significance of this study.
